title: Stroganina
subtitle: Siberian frozen raw fish, shaved thin
excerpt: A whole salmon fillet frozen solid, then shaved into translucent curls with a sharp knife and eaten immediately with coarse salt and black pepper. Siberia's oldest fish preparation, no cooking, no curing, just cold and clean.
sourceNotes: Based on the Siberian and Far Eastern Russian tradition of stroganina as documented in Anya von Bremzen, 'Mastering the Art of Soviet Cooking' (2013) and ethnographic accounts of Yakut and Nenets food culture. The dish predates Russian settlement of Siberia; it originated with indigenous peoples of the far north for whom frozen fish was the primary winter food source. Salmon is a reasonable substitute in Britain for the freshwater white fish (nelma, muksun, chir) of the original.

content.text: Stroganina is one of the most minimal preparations in this collection: a solid-frozen fish, shaved with a sharp knife into thin curls, eaten at once with salt and pepper while still cold. The name comes from the Russian verb strogat, to shave or plane. In Siberia and the Russian Far East, the fish used are freshwater whitefish from Arctic rivers: nelma, muksun, or chir, caught through ice and left to freeze in the open air. In Britain, the closest practical option is a whole salmon fillet bought specifically for this purpose and frozen solid for at least 8 hours.

The fish must be sushi-grade, commercially frozen to -20°C for at least 24 hours before sale, or deep-frozen at home for at least 7 days. Most supermarket salmon labelled 'suitable for eating raw' or 'sashimi-grade' meets this requirement. Do not use refrigerated fish that has not been frozen.

The technique is purely about temperature and angle. The knife shaves across the grain of the fillet, producing translucent, curling slices. Work quickly; the fish softens as it warms. The whole serving process takes under two minutes.

items.name: Salmon fillet
items.prepNote: sushi-grade; frozen solid for at least 8 hours before use

content.text: Place {{salmon-fillet}} of sushi-grade salmon fillet on a tray. Freeze for at least 8 hours until rock solid. If you bought it already deep-frozen, move it to the fridge for about 15 minutes. You want it still hard but not brittle.

Place the frozen fillet skin-side down on a cold board. Hold the knife at a shallow angle and shave thin slices across the fillet, working from one end to the other. The slices should curl as they come free. Arrange immediately on a cold plate. Serve at once with {{sea-salt-flakes}} of coarse salt and {{black-pepper}} of cracked pepper alongside for each person to season their own. Eat immediately, the fish warms and softens quickly.

items.fix: Let the fillet sit at room temperature for 5 to 10 minutes until very slightly softened at the surface, but still hard at the core. A sharp, thin-bladed knife is non-negotiable.
items.cause: Fish too solid, or knife not sharp enough
items.symptom: Slices are thick and jagged rather than thin and curling

items.fix: The fillet must be frozen solid. Keep the board and plate cold. Work quickly once shaving begins.
items.cause: Not frozen thoroughly, or too much thawing time before shaving
items.symptom: Fish is soft and limp rather than firm

items.fix: Only use salmon labelled sushi-grade, sashimi-grade, or fit for eating raw. If in doubt, do not serve raw. Shop-bought deep-frozen fish is usually safe. Fridge-only fish is not.
items.cause: Fish not sushi-grade or bought from an unclear source

content.text: Stroganina comes from the Yakut, Nenets, and other native peoples of Arctic Siberia. Winter air there is cold enough to act as a deep-freeze, and the rivers under the ice hold big stocks of whitefish. Russian settlers from the seventeenth century onwards took up the dish. It is still the signature fish dish of Siberia and the Russian Far East. It travelled west into Soviet food culture as a curiosity. Today it appears on menus in Moscow and St Petersburg as a northern delicacy.
